description: "Fault energies in Fe–Al alloys" presents the variation of antiphase boundary (APB) energies as a function of aluminum content (20–45%) in Fe–Al alloys with different long-range ordered states. The plot contains three distinct data series: solid circles connected by a solid line denote the APBI energy in B2-ordered alloys, which increases steeply from roughly 15 mJ/m² at ~27% Al to about 110 mJ/m² at ~36% Al; solid squares connected by a short-dashed line represent the APBI energy in DO₃-ordered alloys near 25–30% Al, showing modest variation in the range of roughly 65–80 mJ/m²; and open triangles connected by a long-dashed line show the APBII energy in DO₃-ordered alloys in the same composition window, decreasing from about 85 mJ/m² at ~26% Al to about 65 mJ/m² at ~29% Al. The caption states that these data are compiled from literature sources [19–21]. The key contrast is that B2-ordered APBI energies rise dramatically with Al content, whereas DO₃-ordered APBI and APBII energies remain comparatively low and are either weakly varying or decreasing. This figure supports the core conclusion that low APB energies in the DO₃-ordered, low-Al regime cannot keep partial dislocations closely coupled as superdislocations, whereas the high APBI energies in B2-ordered, high-Al alloys favor tightly bound dislocation pairs, directly governing the dislocation configurations and work-hardening response discussed in the text.
